evidence_quotes.quote: Mandatory login with personal data, mandatory internet connection, disruptive updates without quality assurance, mandatory software bloat and potential data theft via Recall, all drove me away from a product I have paid for multiple times - once for each PC or laptop I've bought (each time adding about 100 EUR to the cost of the PC or laptop).
Monthly fees are also becoming more common, replacing the option to buy and own a book or a game.
I dread that the service I subscribe to, to access my books and games will eventually be shut down, making my purchases inaccessible.
I buy my games on Steam, which has DRM (Digital Rights Management).
If Steam goes out of business, I fear the hundreds of games I bought on it (which also cost me thousands of EUR) will become inacccessible to me.
Amazon Kindle recently removed the option to download books I've bought for it, to my PC.

Mandatory login with personal data, mandatory internet connection, disruptive updates without quality assurance, mandatory software bloat and potential data theft via Recall, all drove me away from a product I have paid for multiple times - once for each PC or laptop I've bought (each time adding about 100 EUR to the cost of the PC or laptop).

paragraphs.text: My two hobbies are reading books and playing computer games; they give me a piece of mind in a world that becomes more depressing on a weekly basis. For both of these, the digital medium is slowly replacing the psysical one. Digital copies of books and games are far easier for me to obtain, both in terms of price and availability. Unfortunately, in the digital medium, practices such as planned obsolesence, mandatory online connection, mandatory giving away personal data, are becoming more and more common. Monthly fees are also becoming more common, replacing the option to buy and own a book or a game. For me, this creates a sense of dread, that a book or game I've bought will only be available for a limited time. I wont be able to replay my favorite game, I wont be able to re-read a book I've almost forgotten. I've been able to preserve and enjoy copies of books and games (physical and digital) I deeply care about for decades. I dread that business practices aimed at nothing but profit will remove my ability to preserve books and games I've bought. I dread that the service I subscribe to, to access my books and games will eventually be shut down, making my purchases inaccessible. Already today I can give three examples of this. First, Amazon Kindle recently removed the option to download books I've bought for it, to my PC. If Kindle is discontinued as a product, I fear my books (on which I've spent thousands of EUR) will be lost to me. Second, I buy my games on Steam, which has DRM (Digital Rights Management). Essentially I cannot play my games without Steam running on my PC. If Steam goes out of business, I fear the hundreds of games I bought on it (which also cost me thousands of EUR) will become inacccessible to me. Third and last, I changed my operating system to Linux, not wanting to use Windows anymore due to the anti-consumer practices involved in it. Mandatory login with personal data, mandatory internet connection, disruptive updates without quality assurance, mandatory software bloat and potential data theft via Recall, all drove me away from a product I have paid for multiple times - once for each PC or laptop I've bought (each time adding about 100 EUR to the cost of the PC or laptop). Overall, I do not feel protected by law from the predatory practices of corporations when paying for digital products, neither when buying a product nor when paying for a service. I would be glad if European laws would ensure I can enjoy my purchases for as long as I can preserve them.
